title: Persistent matchmaking
speaker: Uli Bauer
abstract: In this talk, I will give an overview of some recent results motivated by the computation and applications of persistent homology, a theory that creates a bridge between the continuous world of topology and the discrete world of data, and assigns stable and structurally simple invariants to geometric point sets, parametrized over geometric scale. In particular, I will explore why cohomology is so much more efficient in such computations and how it can also be employed to efficiently compute the induced matchings that exhibit the stability of persistence diagrams.
tags: Workshop on Topology: Identifying Order in Complex Systems

proof_stages.label: Persistent-homology and barcode-as-matching setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates Ripsa and persistent homology, recalls filtrations, persistence modules and barcodes, and interprets a barcode as a diagram of partial matchings over the filtration parameter.

proof_stages.label: Interleaving-induced barcode matchings
proof_stages.summary: Interleavings are introduced as natural transformations between filtrations or persistence modules, and the image of an interleaving morphism is used to induce a partial matching of barcodes, giving a constructive route to algebraic stability.

proof_stages.label: Boundary-matrix reduction as persistence pairing
proof_stages.summary: For Rips filtrations refined to simplexwise order, filtered Gaussian elimination of the boundary matrix produces a compatible basis that pairs birth cycles with the chains that kill them.

proof_stages.label: Clearing and cohomological dual computation
proof_stages.summary: The clearing optimization avoids reductions of columns known not to be needed, and dualizing to persistent cohomology makes clearing efficient by moving the unavailable dimension from the top to dimension zero.

proof_stages.label: Relative cohomology and absolute-relative correspondence
proof_stages.summary: Coboundary reduction is identified with persistent relative cohomology, and the short exact sequence of a filtered pair plus its long exact homology sequence relates relative and absolute barcodes via finite and infinite parts.

proof_stages.label: Functorial lifespan decompositions
proof_stages.summary: To handle morphisms rather than just objects, the noncanonical barcode splittings are replaced by functorial lifespan constructions such as finite, infinite, nascent, ancient, and complementary parts.

proof_stages.label: Image persistence from relative cohomology
proof_stages.summary: For an inclusion of filtrations, the desired image-persistence pieces are connected to efficiently computable relative cohomology images by lifespan functors and by monomorphism/epimorphism conditions on the induced limit and colimit maps.

proof_stages.label: Concrete reductions, implicit storage, and computational payoff
proof_stages.summary: The functorial description is translated into matrix reductions, showing that cohomological image persistence with clearing needs fewer reductions, and then into Ripsa implementation shortcuts such as implicit boundary computation and apparent-pair detection.

examples.name: SARS-CoV-2 genome dataset
examples.note: The dataset of distinct COVID genomes illustrates the computational scale and structured, nearly tree-like data for which the improved Ripsa implementation is useful.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies Rips/RIPSA computations to the SARS-CoV-2 genome dataset, estimating skeleton size and discussing the computed H1-features and feasibility

examples.name: Three-by-four rectangle apparent-pair filtration
examples.note: A simple Rips filtration from a rectangular point configuration illustrates how apparent persistence pairs can be detected directly from boundary-matrix pivots and even before enumerating a whole column.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs through the 3-by-4 rectangle Rips boundary ordering, identifies apparent pairs, and traces a specific simplex column to its pivot

examples.name: Recombination and homoplasy as H1-producing events
examples.note: The viral-evolution scenarios explain why one should look for degree-one persistent cycles in otherwise tree-like phylogenetic data, producing the application’s guiding idea.
